system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 8: Resolutions of Chord Cycles

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 8 — Resolutions of Chord Cycles

_Pages 11-11._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## V to I resolution

> the cycle in which we are most interested is from dominant V to tonic I. Now
> the question is, what is the V and the I? The V of a chord demands resolution, the I is
> final. In other words, once you have played the | the ear is satisfied.

**Significance (p. 11).** This defines the fundamental harmonic tension-and-release relationship between dominant and tonic that underpins all subsequent cycle work in the method.

## Dominant as most active chord

> Any chord that you
> play during the course of a cycle demands some kind of temporary resolution until it
> eventually reaches the V (dominant the most active chord of the tonality) and the V re-
> solves into the I

**Significance (p. 11).** This establishes the hierarchical role of the dominant within a chord cycle, explaining why all harmonic motion gravitates toward V before resolving to I.

## I-IV-V-I cycle

> One of the most popular cycles is from I to IV to V and back to 1

**Significance (p. 11).** This names and prioritizes the I-IV-V-I progression as a foundational cycle students must internalize before advancing.

## I-II-V-I cycle

> Another popular example, and the one in which we are most interested, is from I to II ta
> Vv to I

**Significance (p. 11).** This flags the I-II-V-I cycle as the primary focus of the lesson, setting up the substitution concept introduced in lesson 9.

## Minor II substitution preview

> Commit these exercises to memory because in lesson 9 we will approach the art of sub-
> stituting minor II chords for dominant V chords.

**Significance (p. 11).** This is a forward-looking rule that assigns pedagogical weight to memorizing the cycles and directly motivates the substitution technique to be taught next.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
